nativeRange.habitat: Small, slow-moving forest streams, coastal swamps, marshes, and shallow water holes with dense surface vegetation and low flow

diet.notes: Micropredator that tends to prefer small live and frozen foods. Has been documented accepting baby brine shrimp, daphnia, copepods, micro-worms, and crushed micro-pellets or flake. Freeze-dried daphnia and frozen cyclops are commonly accepted. Live or frozen micro-foods are generally recommended for best condition and color.

imageCandidates.notes: By Dornenwolf, 2466x1878. Tack-sharp adult with brilliant blue eye, crisp dark torpedo bands and vivid orange/red/blue fan-shaped rocket tail; all diagnostic features on a vibrant live specimen.
By Cassius Chaerea, 3840x2160. Full-body side profile against dark background showing banded torpedo body and colorful fan tail; fish is a little small in frame but high resolution and clearly ID'd.

By ATuin-hek (public domain), 864x1048. Live specimen at the planted surface showing natural surface-dwelling behavior, red-striped fan tail and bands; slight yellow cast but a good complementary in-tank view.

summary: Tiny West African killifish with bold torpedo-striped body and fan-shaped, brilliantly colored tail. A peaceful nano-tank surface dweller best kept in soft, acidic water with floating plants and gentle filtration.
careNotes:
Unlike Nothobranchius and other annual killifish — which survive dry seasons as dormant eggs buried in mud — the Clown Killifish is a non-annual species that does not undergo this resting phase and tends to live 2 to 5 years in well-maintained aquariums. In the wild, it inhabits small, slow-moving forest streams, coastal swamps, and shallow water holes across Sierra Leone, Guinea, and Liberia, where the water is soft, acidic, and heavily shaded by surface vegetation.

The species is also sold and found in older literature under the name Pseudepiplatys annulatus, reflecting a period when it was placed in a separate genus. Current taxonomy returns it to Epiplatys, though the older name remains common in the hobby.

Water chemistry: the Clown Killifish generally does best in soft, acidic water. A pH of 4.0 to 6.5 is commonly preferred in the hobby; the full tolerance extends to around 7.0, but harder or more alkaline conditions tend to reduce vitality and color over time. Soft water (gH 1–8) and low carbonate hardness (kH 0–4) are generally advised. Filtration should be gentle — a sponge filter or small air-driven filter is commonly recommended to maintain the near-still surface conditions the species prefers.

Unlike most killifish, the Clown Killifish has short, fan-shaped fins that somewhat limit jumping ability, and it is generally considered a lower escape risk than typical killifish. A secure lid is still advisable as a precaution and to limit evaporation.

Males have been known to display toward each other with fin-spreading but rarely cause damage. A trio (one male to two females) or a small group tends to work well in a 30–40 L planted nano tank. Suitable tankmates include other small, peaceful nano species — micro-rasboras, small tetras, pygmy corydoras, and adult dwarf shrimp. Boisterous or aggressive species and fin-nippers are best avoided, as are very small shrimp fry, which may be eaten.
breedingNotes: Breeding has been reported as moderate in difficulty. This species tends to scatter eggs among fine-leaved or floating plants; Java moss, hornwort, or spawning mops are commonly used. Eggs generally hatch in approximately 10-14 days at typical tank temperatures. Fry are tiny and have been reported to require infusoria or commercial first-foods such as vinegar eels and paramecium before graduating to newly hatched brine shrimp. Separating adults from eggs or raising fry in a dedicated container is generally advised as adults will consume eggs and fry. A small group can produce spawns regularly under good conditions.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, taxonomy, native range, adult size (4.0 cm max), temperature (23-25°C), pH (6.5-7.0), and hardness (dH ≤5). SeriouslyFish (first listed secondary) returned HTTP 520 and was not usable.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Third secondary used after SeriouslyFish (HTTP 520) and Practical Fishkeeping (HTTP 403). Confirmed native range (Guinea, Liberia, Sierra Leone), adult size (3-3.5 cm), temperature (20-26°C), pH (4-7), and compatibility notes.

fish.breedingNotes: Plant-spawner; fry require infusoria or micro-foods initially. See breedingNotes for full details.
